title: Statistics & Regression
blurb: Mean, standard deviation, five-number summaries, ogives, least-squares regression and correlation.

questions.context: The daily taxi commute times (in minutes) of 10 workers travelling from Soweto to Johannesburg CBD are recorded: $32; 45; 28; 51; 39; 47; 35; 42; 56; 40$

parts.q: Calculate the mean commute time.
Calculate the standard deviation of the commute times.
How many workers have a commute time within one standard deviation of the mean?
solution: $\therefore$ 6 workers lie within one standard deviation of the mean.

questions.context: A takeaway in Durban records the number of bunny chows sold per day over 11 days. The sorted data is: $12; 15; 18; 21; 24; 27; 33; 39; 46; 52; 60$

parts.q: Write down the five-number summary of the data.
solution: Median: $n=11$, so the median is the 6th value: $M=27$
$Q_1$ is the median of the lower 5 values (3rd value): $Q_1=18$
$Q_3$ is the median of the upper 5 values (9th value): $Q_3=46$

parts.q: Calculate the interquartile range (IQR).
The data is represented on a box-and-whisker diagram. Comment on the skewness of the data, giving a reason based on the quartiles.
solution: Since $Q_3-M>M-Q_1$, the data is skewed to the right (positively skewed).
parts.answer: Skewed to the right (positively skewed), since $Q_3-M=19>M-Q_1=9$

parts.q: By using the rule that an outlier lies more than $1{,}5\times IQR$ beyond a quartile, show that the value $60$ is NOT an outlier.
solution: Since $60<88$, the value $60$ is not an outlier.
parts.answer: $60<Q_3+1{,}5\times IQR=88$, so $60$ is not an outlier

questions.context: The finishing times of 80 runners in a 10 km fun run in Cape Town are grouped in the table below (time $t$ in minutes): $30\le t<40$: 6 runners; $40\le t<50$: 14 runners; $50\le t<60$: 26 runners; $60\le t<70$: 20 runners; $70\le t<80$: 10 runners; $80\le t<90$: 4 runners. A cumulative frequency graph (ogive) is drawn for this data.

parts.q: Write down the cumulative frequencies for the six intervals.
solution: (Each value is plotted at the upper boundary of its interval on the ogive.)

parts.q: Write down the modal class of the data.
solution: The class with the highest frequency (26 runners) is $50\le t<60$.

parts.q: Use the ogive to estimate the median finishing time.
solution: The median is at the $\frac{80}{2}=40$th runner, which lies in the interval $50\le t<60$.
Reading across from 40 on the cumulative frequency axis: $M\approx 50+\frac{40-20}{26}\times 10\approx 57{,}7$ minutes
parts.answer: $M\approx 57{,}7$ minutes (accept $57$–$58$ minutes read from the ogive)

parts.q: Estimate how many runners finished in under 65 minutes.
solution: At $t=65$ (halfway through the interval $60\le t<70$): cumulative frequency $\approx 46+\frac{5}{10}\times 20=46+10=56$
$\therefore$ approximately 56 runners finished in under 65 minutes.

parts.q: What percentage of the runners took 70 minutes or longer?

questions.context: A spaza shop owner in Polokwane records the maximum daily temperature ($x$, in °C) and the number of cold drinks sold ($y$) on 8 days: $(18; 40),\ (21; 44),\ (24; 52),\ (26; 57),\ (29; 66),\ (31; 68),\ (33; 75),\ (35; 80)$

parts.q: Determine the equation of the least-squares regression line $\hat{y}=a+bx$. Round $a$ and $b$ to four decimal places.
solution: By calculator (or from $b=\frac{n\sum xy-\sum x\sum y}{n\sum x^2-(\sum x)^2}$ with $n=8$, $\sum x=217$, $\sum y=482$, $\sum xy=13671$, $\sum x^2=6133$):

parts.q: Use the regression line to predict the number of cold drinks sold on a day when the maximum temperature is 28 °C.
Calculate the correlation coefficient $r$, rounded to four decimal places.
Interpret the value of $r$ in the context of this data.
solution: There is a very strong positive linear relationship between the maximum daily temperature and the number of cold drinks sold: as temperature increases, sales increase.
parts.answer: Very strong positive linear correlation between temperature and cold drink sales

parts.q: The owner wants to use the regression line to predict sales on a day with a maximum temperature of 45 °C. Explain why this prediction would be unreliable.
solution: $45$ °C lies well outside the range of the observed data ($18$ °C to $35$ °C).
This is extrapolation — the linear trend cannot be assumed to continue beyond the data range, so the prediction is unreliable.
parts.answer: It is extrapolation: 45 °C is outside the observed range (18 °C to 35 °C), so the linear model may not hold

questions.context: A club cricketer's runs in 8 innings are: $34; 12; 45; x; 28; 51; 9; 41$. The mean of the 8 innings is 32 runs.

parts.q: Show that $x=36$.
Calculate the standard deviation of the 8 innings.
solution: By calculator: $\sigma\approx 14{,}04$ runs (rounded to 2 decimal places)

parts.q: In a 9th innings the cricketer scores 130 runs. Without full recalculation, explain the effect of this score on (i) the mean and (ii) the standard deviation of the data.
solution: (i) $130$ is far above every other value, so the mean increases substantially (from $32$ to $\frac{288+130}{9}\approx 42{,}89$).
(ii) The outlier lies far from the mean, so the spread of the data increases — the standard deviation increases (to $\approx 33{,}52$).
parts.answer: Both increase: the mean rises to $\approx 42{,}89$ and the standard deviation increases sharply

parts.q: After the 9th innings, which measure of central tendency — the mean or the median — better represents the cricketer's typical score? Justify your answer.
solution: The median (which only moves from $35$ to $36$) better represents the typical score, because the median is resistant to the outlier of $130$, whereas the mean is pulled up to $\approx 42{,}89$ — higher than 7 of the 9 scores.
parts.answer: The median, because it is not distorted by the outlier of 130

questions.context: During a drought, the daily water usage ($x$, in litres) of 120 households in Mbombela was recorded. An ogive (cumulative frequency curve) was drawn from the data. The cumulative frequency table used for the ogive reads: $x<20$: 12 households; $x<40$: 42; $x<60$: 76; $x<80$: 102; $x<100$: 116; $x<120$: 120.

parts.q: How many households used at least 40 but less than 60 litres of water per day?
Write down the modal class of the data.
solution: Frequencies per interval (differences of the cumulative frequencies): $12;\ 30;\ 34;\ 26;\ 14;\ 4$
The highest frequency (34) belongs to $40\le x<60$.

parts.q: Use the ogive to estimate the median daily water usage.
solution: The median is at the $\frac{120}{2}=60$th household, which lies in the interval $40\le x<60$ (cumulative frequency goes from 42 to 76).
Reading across from 60 on the cumulative frequency axis: $M\approx 40+\frac{60-42}{34}\times 20\approx 50{,}6$ litres
parts.answer: $M\approx 50{,}6$ litres (accept $50$–$51$ litres read from the ogive)

parts.q: Estimate the interquartile range of the data.
solution: $Q_1$ is at the $\frac{120}{4}=30$th household, in $20\le x<40$: $Q_1\approx 20+\frac{30-12}{30}\times 20=32$ litres
$Q_3$ is at the $90$th household, in $60\le x<80$: $Q_3\approx 60+\frac{90-76}{26}\times 20\approx 70{,}8$ litres

parts.q: The municipality fines any household that uses more than 90 litres per day. Estimate how many households will be fined.

questions.context: The number of minutes that 12 Grade 12 learners in Bloemfontein spent on social media one evening is: $28;\ 35;\ 22;\ 41;\ 5;\ 30;\ 44;\ 25;\ 32;\ 38;\ 18;\ 95$

parts.q: Determine the five-number summary of the data.
solution: $n=12$, so the median is the average of the 6th and 7th values: $M=\frac{30+32}{2}=31$
$Q_1=\frac{22+25}{2}=23{,}5$ (median of the lower 6 values) and $Q_3=\frac{38+41}{2}=39{,}5$ (median of the upper 6 values)

parts.q: Use the rule that an outlier lies more than $1{,}5\times IQR$ beyond a quartile to show that $95$ is an outlier, but $5$ is not.
solution: Upper fence: $Q_3+1{,}5\times IQR=39{,}5+24=63{,}5$. Since $95>63{,}5$, the value $95$ IS an outlier.
Lower fence: $Q_1-1{,}5\times IQR=23{,}5-24=-0{,}5$. Since $5>-0{,}5$, the value $5$ is NOT an outlier.

parts.q: The data is represented on a box-and-whisker diagram. Comment on the skewness of the data, giving a reason.
solution: $Q_3-M=39{,}5-31=8{,}5$ and $M-Q_1=31-23{,}5=7{,}5$, and the maximum (95) lies far beyond $Q_3$, giving a much longer right whisker.
$\therefore$ the data is skewed to the right (positively skewed).
parts.answer: Skewed to the right (positively skewed) — the right whisker is much longer and $Q_3-M>M-Q_1$

questions.context: The daily wages (in rand) of the 7 workers at a car wash in Tembisa are: $170;\ 190;\ 200;\ 220;\ 230;\ 250;\ 280$

parts.q: Calculate the mean and the standard deviation of the daily wages.
Each worker receives an increase of R35 per day. Write down the new mean and the new standard deviation.
solution: Adding a constant to every value shifts the mean by that constant: new mean $=220+35=R255$
The spread of the data is unchanged: new $\sigma\approx R34{,}64$ (unchanged)

parts.q: The owner instead considers increasing every wage by 10%. Determine the new mean and the new standard deviation for this option.
solution: Multiplying every value by $1{,}1$ scales both measures: new mean $=220\times 1{,}1=R242$

parts.q: Explain why the standard deviation stays the same for the R35 increase but changes for the 10% increase.
solution: Adding R35 shifts every value by the same amount, so each deviation $x_i-\bar{x}$ is unchanged — the spread, and therefore $\sigma$, stays the same.
Multiplying by $1{,}1$ stretches every deviation by a factor of $1{,}1$, so the spread increases and $\sigma$ is multiplied by $1{,}1$ as well.
parts.answer: A constant shift leaves the deviations from the mean unchanged; a percentage increase scales the deviations (and $\sigma$) by the same factor

questions.context: A petrol station in Benoni records the number of hours of load-shedding per day ($x$) and the number of litres of petrol sold for generators ($y$) on 8 days: $(2; 35),\ (3; 48),\ (4; 52),\ (5; 66),\ (6; 70),\ (7; 85),\ (8; 92),\ (10; 110)$

parts.q: Determine the equation of the least-squares regression line $\hat{y}=a+bx$. Round $a$ and $b$ to four decimal places.
solution: By calculator (or from $b=\frac{n\sum xy-\sum x\sum y}{n\sum x^2-(\sum x)^2}$ with $n=8$, $\sum x=45$, $\sum y=558$, $\sum xy=3603$, $\sum x^2=303$):

parts.q: Predict the number of litres of petrol sold for generators on a day with 9 hours of load-shedding.
Write down the value of the correlation coefficient $r$, rounded to four decimal places.
Interpret the value of $r$ in the context of this data.
solution: There is a very strong positive linear relationship between the hours of load-shedding and the litres of generator petrol sold: more load-shedding, more petrol sold.
parts.answer: Very strong positive linear correlation between load-shedding hours and generator petrol sales

parts.q: Explain why the prediction made in the earlier part of this question can be considered reliable.
solution: $x=9$ lies inside the range of the observed data ($2$ to $10$ hours), so it is interpolation — and $r\approx 0{,}9958$ shows the linear model fits the data very well.
parts.answer: It is interpolation ($9$ lies within the data range $2$–$10$) and the correlation is very strong
